## POST 1 — X (Twitter) Post: Building in Public

━━━ POST ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PLATFORM: X (Twitter)
TYPE: post
PILLAR: 1 — Building in Public
SERIES: Building Acme Corp
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Just shipped our new onboarding flow. Took 3 weeks, rewrote it twice. The first version was overengineered, the second was too simple. Third time was the charm — 40% fewer drop-offs in the first 48 hours.

Sometimes the answer is just... less steps.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CTA: What's your onboarding conversion rate?

## POST 2 — LinkedIn Post: Founder Insight

━━━ POST ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PLATFORM: LinkedIn
TYPE: post
PILLAR: 4 — Founder Journey
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

6 months ago we almost killed the B2B product.

Revenue was flat, churn was climbing, and every meeting ended with "interesting but we already have a solution for that."

Then one customer said something that changed everything: "I don't need another tool. I need this one to talk to the 4 I already have."

So we stopped building features and started building integrations. 3 months later, B2B is 60% of revenue.

The lesson isn't "listen to customers" — everyone says that. The lesson is that sometimes the feature your customer needs isn't a feature at all. It's a bridge.

What's the most unexpected pivot you've made based on customer feedback?

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CTA: Comment below
